pages.text: $(o)"Whatever can be written can be erased."$()$(br2)The $(l)Nexus$() is the crystalline residue-plane formed when Transcendence shattered — a slice of reality where "to write" was "to make permanent".

pages.title: Naming the Five Laws
pages.text: The names of the five Nexuses are warnings in themselves:$(br2)$(l)$(#5cf)Binding$()$() — your casts slow$(br)$(l)$(#0f0)Scarcity$()$() — mana regen withers$(br)$(l)$(#f55)Mercy$()$() — buffs shrink, levels drop$(br)$(l)$(#fa0)Fragility$()$() — you take more damage$(br)$(l)$(#d5f)Isolation$()$() — mana wells fall near-silent$(br2)They target not the mage — they are the $(l)layer of reality$() itself constraining you.

pages.title: Why It's Designed This Way
pages.text: The ancients found: to truly $(l)break$() a law, one must $(l)accept$() its price.$(br2)Destroying a Nexus removes the limit — but as "compensation", the entire world bears the cost.$(br2)$(o)"We did not break the laws. We merely transferred them."$() — confession of an anonymous Dominion mage

pages.title: Cost of Total Destruction
pages.text: $(l)$(4)Destroying all five$()$() does not grant invincibility — it enrages the $(l)Transcendence Avatar$().$(br2)All five world-costs stack. The Avatar starts at phase 3, full-revives at phase 4, with ×3 spell power.$(br2)$(o)"You thought this was the endgame. This is Transcendence finally noticing you."$()
